text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Angela Smith
Consultant Neurologist
Centre for Epilepsy
King’s College Hospital, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS

Dear Dr Angela Smith,

Sam Taylor, DOB: 22-07-1992, Hospital No: K842771 NHS No. 9081764231
42 Meadow Lane, Deptford, London, SE8 4QX

Diagnosis:

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy, with both generalised and focal seizure types reported. Prior trials of Levetiracetam and Carbamazepine were limited by mood side effects; current regimen includes Lamotrigine and Clobazam.

Aetiology:

Unclear; no structural lesion on prior MRI (2019) and normal perinatal history. Family history negative for epilepsy.

Other Medical Problems:

Mild iron-deficiency anaemia under GP review; seasonal allergic rhinitis. No known drug allergies.

Current Clinical Summary:

Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two days. Rescue medication was required once in June and once in August. The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review. The primary carer reports fatigue related to night-time monitoring and coordinating rescue plans; we discussed pacing strategies and the option of carer support assessment, which the patient and carer will consider. The patient notes that generalised events are usually preceded by brief focal aware symptoms (rising epigastric sensation and facial tingling) and that VNS swipes occasionally abort progression.

Medications:

- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily (same brand)
- Clobazam 10 mg nocte, with an additional 5 mg PRN for clusters (max 20 mg/day)
- VNS active since 2022; patient-initiated magnet swipes as trained
- Rescue: Buccal midazolam 10 mg as per protocol (used June and August as above)

Management Plan:

- Continue Lamotrigine and Clobazam at current doses; maintain single-brand Lamotrigine.
- Continue VNS with current parameters; reinforce appropriate magnet use as per epilepsy nurse guidance.
- Provide carer information pack and signpost to local carers’ support services; patient and primary carer to self-refer for a carers’ assessment via the local authority.
- Bloods today: FBC, ferritin, U&Es, LFTs, vitamin D given reported fatigue and known anaemia.
- Epilepsy nurse telephone review in 8 weeks to check cluster pattern and carer fatigue; consultant review in 6 months or sooner if clusters increase or rescue medication is needed more than twice per month.
- Safety: Up-to-date seizure action plan shared with school/workplace; patient declines changes to driving status as not currently driving.

We discussed that while seizure clusters persist, there has been no overall rise in frequency since the last review. The patient and carer prefer to avoid medication escalation at this stage and will focus on optimising VNS swiping and sleep routines. They will contact the service if generalised events increase or if rescue use exceeds agreed thresholds.

Yours sincerely,

entities.text: Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two days.

Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two days. Rescue medication was required once in June and once in August. The patient has increased VNS swiping, but there has been no overall rise in seizure frequency since the last review

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two days." This provides a clear temporal frequency of seizure occurrence as clusters every 2 days. The number of seizures within each cluster is not specified, so the most precise normalised label without per-cluster details is 1 per 2 day.
seizure_frequency_number: Seizures remain relatively stable, typically occurring in clusters every two days.
